sentences.text: Yeah, so we was from the [?
Latvia. ?] My other sister's husband was a butcher, and he, too, was working, because once in awhile they brought up some dead horses in.
And they were dividing them between the population in the ghetto.
They gave them, once in awhile, a little piece of meat.
So he brought us always a piece of horse meat.
I never knew it was horse meat.
We never ate horse meat or my mother cooked it, and we had a meal.
So afterwards, he said, it's horse meat.
And I said, it was very good, because when you're hungry everything tastes good.
So you know, life in the ghetto was very, very hard.
Very hard.
Very hard.
During that time, did you hear about any concentration camps?
No.
We didn't hear nothing.
We knew there was-- we heard about Warsaw.
We heard about people came back from Bahlsen.
They came to us.
They had relatives.
So they smuggled themselves out from the ghetto, and they came back and they were telling us the terrible stories of how people died.
The time that they came, they didn't start here to take out the Jews to the horrible places.
So they were just telling stories, what was going on, how people die, how people are hungry-- what's going on.
It was just terrible.
So we said, we have our misery, too.
And it was, you know, things, but we had to take it 'till, you know, this happened in 1942-- [INAUDIBLE] in October. 1942 in October, yeah-- In October.
Can I go back?
I have something to say?
I want to say something about the ghetto.
Between we was in the ghetto, whoever did something bad, people got punched.
So I remember a most tragic thing.
There was a young man who was smuggling, and they catched him.
And this, I will never forget as long as I live.
They took this young man by his hands, they tied him up to a wagon, and they went across the whole ghetto, around the ghetto, the wagon with the horse, it was going.
And he was behind the wheels chained up.
And his body was on the ground, and there was just rocks.
It was not like anybody else.
There was no-- not a road.
There was just a dirt road.
And I saw this.
I saw this.
And they was dragging him.
And when they stopped, this person was, I think, still alive.
Their whole body was just torn apart-- torn, his legs, his hands, his back, because they was towing like that.
And they shot him.
At the end, he was still alive.
They shot him.
And they took him to the cemetery, who knows where this was.
And except this, there was people who did other things.
So they brought them to the ghetto, and they brought him back, they called everybody out, and they hanged him.
There was a case with a man who was married to a Gentile person, and he said, he's not Jewish.
So they showed him he's a Jew, and right away they hanged him.
Because everybody probably knows, Hitler was looking at three, four generations who had a little blood, Jewish blood.
All these people all got sent to the ghetto, or sent to the concentration.
Or he plain killed them.
No.
My clothes.
No, nobody was wearing--
Yeah, I was wearing a star with the name, UDA.
A yellow star-- everybody.
On my chest.
On the front of my chest I was wearing the star.
From the-- there was somebody who was making them-- a tailor, or somebody from the-- not the ghetto, from the committee.
They was giving out of the stars.
It was a Jewish star with UDA on it.
They got worse.
When things got worse, they came probably-- I don't know how they did.
I remember in October, 1942, they start doing the things-- calling out people and putting them on trains.
The Germans.
The Germans came into the ghetto, and they-- no, they didn't came to the ghetto.
They just came to the committee.
They said tomorrow, we need 2,000 people.
So first went the poor, then the sick, and all the children who was left alone that was living in the house, special [INAUDIBLE].
And they took all these people.
And this was the first transport who went from the ghetto.
They took them to the trains-- walking.
It was a few kilometers to go, walking distance.
They went there, then they put them on the trains, and we never heard about them.
Three weeks later, the same procedure started again.
This was going 'til December.
So I was from the last.
So one half of the ghetto was already completely empty.
You could go in the houses.
There was the [INAUDIBLE], you couldn't find nothing there.
You could find, you know, a little furniture or a pillow.
Everybody, whoever, somebody had something, people took with them-- they said, they're going to go to our working camp, who knows where?
So there was nothing left.
So there came the day the whole ghetto had to be cleaned out.
No more Jews.
So we was from the last transport.
When was that?
This was December the 14th, probably.
Something like that.
Took them probably three days to come to Auschwitz.
So they put us on trains.
Yeah, we was marching to the town-- it was winter, cold.
Luckily we had the best clothes, we put on them.
I remember like today, I had a pair of leather boots, and I had a brown jacket.
I still remember what I was wearing.
And I had a little rucksack.
I didn't have nothing-- everything was gone.
So everybody had a little rucksack.
What was there in the rucksack?
Probably a change of clothes, things like that.
And my sister was there, the older sisters, and my brother, and my father, and my mother.
They put us all together in the train.
This was not an open train.
This was a regular train.
We were all sitting together, even they put more people.
Even in the-- excuse me-- in the bathroom, there was people sitting there.
And so we was traveling.
We went through from Plotz, we went to Warsaw, and Warsaw went to Czestochowa.
In Czestochowa we stopped, and we looked out the window-- see, we were so thirsty, even in winter.
We didn't know, whoever had a piece of bread, we ate on the train.
And people were standing on the train, and people were [INAUDIBLE], thirsty.

And the Polish people, whoever was there, they were afraid, because the Germans saw the way they jumped out from the train, and they was watching, barking for the dogs, watching the trains, nobody to run away.
So this was a day and a half or two days.
It took us another day.
The same evening, at night, about 3 o'clock at night, we arrived to Auschwitz.
It was night, cold.
And from far away, I saw a lot of lights-- lights, looked like a big city.
You know, lights, lights, quiet.
It was at night.
So they told us, they came out to the trains, we looked around, said, oh, must be something nice here.
So right away they told us, leave everything in that train.
Don't touch nothing, just come out.
So I went out, my sister Hanka, my other sisters went out.
And the older sister had two children.
So I was holding the older one when she came down from the wagon.
I was holding this in the other hand, I had slight piece bread.
This I took with me.
I was so [INAUDIBLE].
So when I was standing with her, and I said to her, Frida, here.
Hold the piece of bread.
The little baby's going to be hungry.
You're going to have something to eat.
So they told us to put five in a row.
And the Germans, everybody was there.
So you know, a lot of people, dogs, so many Germans going around, screaming.
People was jumping, sick people, because people couldn't go out on the trains.
There was so much commotion going on.
So after awhile, the Germans they start looking.
They start looking.
I didn't see Hanka.
I was just with with my older sister.
I didn't nobody.
I lost them.
And the German came to me-- I don't know who he was or what he was.
He was a tall, fat guy.
And he looked me straight in the eyes and asked me how old are you?
So I told him.
So he said, this way.
And to the sister, and the children-- So before he came, I let the hand of my sister's child go, because whoever had a child didn't have a chance.
This was the time that I came-- nobody had a chance, even you was young.
Did you know that?
Afterwards.
Nobody had a child who came into the camp.
So I let him go, and he sent me to the other side.
And other people went on the other side.
And the men was right away separated.
When we came out from the train, the men had to go to one side, and the women the other side.
So we didn't know where the men are, where the father, where the brother, nobody.
We didn't know nothing.
My nephew's, nephew.
I just let go.
Something, I let go of the hand.
And the German, the SS man came to me and asked me what time is, and made with his finger, this way.
So I went this way.
And to the rest, he said this way.
So this transport consisted of around 2,000 people.
We came in, 256 women, and some 500 men.
How I know men?
Because afterwards I was reading the history from Plonsk, so they're saying there was some 500 men.
There was most of them, everybody young-- and the same was between the women.
So they told us to start marching, marching.
We start marching-- it was not far away from Birkenau, Birkenau-Auschwitz.
Birkenau consisted, when they came to the gate, they opened up the gate-- a big, big gate.
You were thinking you were to a paradise-- barracks.
You just saw buildings, buildings.
There was just-- in this time-- there was just two places there.
For men, one, and one for women.
There was an A.
Everything was under A-B-C-- so the A was for the women, the camp, and the B was for the men.
So they tooked us in.
And straight we went to the bath house.
It was a building by itself.
There were toilets there.
And there was rooms.
They told us to undress.
They put everything neat, and who knew where it would go.
So everybody put away nice their clothes.
And nobody had nothing already, just the clothes on them.
And they told us to go to another room.
The other room, there was a lot of steps, and to sit down on the steps.
And they said, here, they're going to shave us, and they're going to do the other things.
So we went in the other rooms.
And a few minutes later, the men came in, not women.
Everybody was naked.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And the men came in, they told us to spread our legs, to spread our hands, they shaved us wherever there was a hair on our body.
They shaved us.
The instruments that they was holding, they were probably used already on thousands and thousands of people.
The blood that was flowing from everybody-- from their heads, from their arm pits from everybody.
And the humiliation, the feelings-- this is a most terrible thing that can happen to a young girl.
When somebody-- you know, you've never undressed for nobody, just for yourself, and here comes bullies.
Who knows, maybe not.
I don't know what kind of men these were.
This was the things that they had to do.
They shaved us.
After the shavings, they put some cold water on us.
And after the water, you know, nothing-- nothing to dry up.
No towels, nothing to dry up.
They put some powder.
Oh, they went with a little machine and they was spraying, a sprayer.
A big man was going and pumping and spraying everybody's heads.
And they start giving us clothes.
The clothing was from already people who died.
They brought it in-- nobody asked your size, they just handed it over to you.
I don't really remember-- I remember I got a dress, and I remember I got a coat.
My head was nothing.
They gave us a piece of cloth to put on the head.
The shoes, they didn't match to my feet.
I was afraid.
So I'm staying and looking at my sister.
She looks at me, and I looked-- and nobody could recognize themself.
I said, my gosh.
And I see all the things staying there.
Nobody touched them.
The clothes, when we took them-- it was in another room.
So I picked myself up, and I jumped in the other room.
Really, I didn't think, you know, they could kill me.
And I grabbed my shoes, my boots-- I was still standing there-- I grabbed my boots.
So I was afraid they're going to see I have a beautiful black, leather boots.
So I took the knife-- I found a knife there-- and I cut down the upper part from the boots, and I put on just the bottoms.
I had my shoes-- It was from the [INAUDIBLE].
And so after a few hours, this took, you know, they had to tattoo us.
And this was going after A, B, C-- so my name is under the K, so it took a long time.
So when everybody was tattooed, they divided us, a part of us, a hundred they took to another place.
Really not to Birkenau, to a satellite from Birkenau.
And me and my sister and all the other people, they tooked us out from the bath, and they tooked us to our barrack.
The barrack's number was number eight.
And this was during the day, the next day, but during the day.
When they opened up the door, and they put us in-- in this barrack-- the barrack was just made from brick-- there was nothing else.
There was just brick barrack.
And these barracks, before the war, they were stables for horses.
The beds was like three flats-- a bottom, a middle, and a top.
And you came in, it was the same thing-- it was like a square in the middle, and with this side like straights-- this side and this side was going the beds, and filled it again.
And the middle was, too.
So when I came in, to the first bed, there was a lady there and she was-- she was crying.
And she said, oy, oy, you know.
And I went to her, and she showed me she wants water.
Water?
I didn't know nothing that's going on.
First thing I ask, where's my mother?
So I said, see the chimneys?
They're murdered, OK, finished.
Then nobody see her.
You're here.
Nobody see her.
So this lady said to me-- she wants drink.
And she couldn't speak even Jewish, I think, a little-- she was from Belgium.
And she came before me-- a few months before.
They took some transports from Belgium, they brought them in.
So I ask where to drink water?
So she told me how sick she was, completely swollen, completely.
She said to me, the minute I came in, she gave me a little direction.
She said go where the big kitchen is.
There was a big building, and there was cooking there.
There is, outside, a leak.
Water was leaking.
You go look, you're going to find it.
And there, maybe you're going to find water.
If not, she said, go to the kitchen.
And there, come supper time there was 50-gallons barrels.
The girls, some of them came, they couldn't carry-- the girl who was in charge, [INAUDIBLE] she was the foreman-- the foreman for the camp.
She had to come to the kitchen, and she had to sign.
She came for the coffee.
So I was standing there-- [INAUDIBLE] I just came-- and she said to ask me, do I want to carry?
Me and another one.
So this was a big barrel, and the barrel had a piece of wood in the arms.
So I put it like this, and she said, do you want to bring it to my block?
She walked with us.
And I had already, when we came into the block, everybody got a bowl.
This bowl, it was for everything.
If you lost this bowl, you lost your life.
So I was holding my brown bowl under my arm, and we walked.
And she tooked us to the end of the block, on the whole they was calling the [GERMAN]-- the street from the camp.
And she opened up her barrack, and she said to bring in.
What I saw there, I couldn't imagine can happen in life.
This was already a block of half-dead people.
All the people who couldn't work, or people who couldn't-- they were sick-- they brought them in.
And this block had a fence, a big, big fence, so nobody could run out, because they're sick people, that maybe they couldn't run out.
So we went in and there was in the yard-- there was a big yard-- dead people and rats-- rats walking on them.
Inside, she didn't let us in.
It was dark, no windows.
We just heard-- we heard how people were [INAUDIBLE], making noise-- 'water,' 'water.' So we put down the tea and the coffee, and she went and she gave us a big bowl-- you know, they're the world.
So I was so happy, and I ran home.
You know, I went home.
And I gave it to this lady, because she told me what to do.
And she said, the long I'm going to live, I'm never going to forget.
The next day she was dead anyways.
She died.
So she told me before she died, she said, she's from Belgium.
She left a child to a nun-- she gave away to her church.
She never told me the name.
She never told me the name from the child.
The only thing is that I know I did a good deed.
She had something to drink, and I helped myself, too.
And she died.
It was the next day or a day.
She couldn't go to work.
When she was already on the-- on the bed, she was on the end of her life.
So afterwards, they assigned us to a place inside.
So we got a bed between-- in-- in the middle-- the middle there was, 10, I think, 10 girls.
In this consisted-- how many feet?
Five feet-- not even, maybe five feet.
So we remember we went inside-- you couldn't sit.
You had to go lay down like that.
Like herrings-- everybody was, you know.
And the next day, at 3 or 4 o'clock in the morning there was knocking, you know, screaming, you know-- [? appel, ?] [? appel. ?] To dress up.
Dress up.
Nobody took off the clothes.
Nobody-- we had to go out.
And it was early.
Very, very early in the morning.
We had to bring all the people outside-- all the sick, and all the dead.
Everybody had to be counted.
It could take us hours, and hours, and hours-- if one person was missing.
I couldn't understand in the beginning.
I didn't know nothing, you know.
So in that way, from the [? appel, ?] they gave us-- inside, we had the tea, I don't remember, really.
And they assigned us to go to work.
Where work?
What kind of work?
Who knew?
But there was 100, 50, 100, 200 girls-- everybody who could work had to move out from the camp.
So they tooked us to work So we marched, and we worked, we worked, we came-- we came to a quarry.
This was the first, if I remember.
We had to go down to a big hole, they give us big picks-- picks and we had to knock in the walls to break up the stones.
And when the stones was little, we had to put them in little wagons, and bring them outside from the quarry.
And the work it was just-- just miserable-- just miserable.
We still had a little strength, you know-- still, you came from the ghetto, you still was, you know, completely-- you didn't know what's going on with you.
And this was going on, you know, slowly, slowly, you got used to it.
You start, you know, comprehend where you are, and what's going on.
You looked around, you found some people from your hometown who came before you, and they was telling us terrible, terrible stories.
They said, you have to make the best.
Try not to get beaten from the Germans, from the SS, because when they gonna put-- they're going to start beating you, or you are not working, or you're doing something-- you are lost.
Had to listen.
So me, Hanka, and some other girls right away we became-- all the girls who slept together with us-- became like sisters.
Really it was five-- not all of them.
Me and Hanka, and three more.
They were from not far-- they came from all the same ghetto.
And we said, you know, whatever somebody is going to have, we're going to share with each other.
And really, we did this, you know.
So around everybody in the beginning, nobody-- nobody had a good job, nobody had nothing.
We start learning what's going on then.
So we went to work, you know, we went to the quarries.
We went to other work-- like we was making-- we went to the water, and we had to straighten out the lakes.
So the work was really-- the work had nothing for them.
They didn't have nothing, they just-- we went to work just to kill us.
So the next work, it was-- we went on a farm, and there was houses, standing brick houses.
So they gave us, again, the picks, and we had to take down, one by one, the red bricks.
And the girls-- the one who took them down-- there was another commander who was cleaning them.
So we didn't understand why they need bricks, you know.
People was here to take apart the houses.
So from these bricks-- so the people who worked the sonderkomando-- sonderkommando, this was a group of people who worked in the crematoriums.
And then, in the time when we came, they were still building.
They was building the crematoriums.
So all the bricks that we took down from the houses and we cleaned, the men came to us, and took this away.
So we went from one house to the next, until we used up all the houses there.
So they took us to another place.
So between, [INAUDIBLE], you know, men came in-- the French Jews, other Jews, there was a-- there was a couple there.
And, you know, he was, you know, talking to us, saying just about nobody-- nobody could-- just with our eyes, we was communicating with our eyes.
And he was very nice, you know.
Sometimes he brought something he picked up from his pocket, because he was working over the dead, so always found something to eat.
So sometimes we got, you know-- we was working for Polish guys.
But other one gave us an onion, one gave us a piece of bread.
So we start making contact with people.
So after this, you know, after this kommando, we start going, yeah-- the weather start getting a little warmer, we start going out to another kommando.
And this, I could not understand-- we was making roads-- roads.
This was like all around Birkenau-Auschwitz there was no roads, because it was farmland.
So they was living in villas, the Germans, and they was driving the automobiles.
They needed a road.
So they brought [INAUDIBLE] trucks, and they opened up the trucks, and everything came down like coal.
Go ahead.
Like coal, and material.
So I picked up this material, I said, why is this so sticky?
Sticky material.
So the guy told us, the bones from the dead.
And there was big, big pieces, so we had to take our hammers and crush it.
We was making roads.
And afterwards, we had hammers and was going around and hammering down-- these were the bones from our people.
